topic: Someone discovers their partner has been writing a novel about them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The late-night glow of the desk lamp cast long, sharp shadows across the room. I was ostensibly reading a book, but my eyes kept drifting. A stack of loose papers, annotated with meticulous handwriting, sat beside my partner’s laptop. Tonight, the words were different. They weren't grocery lists or work notes. They were descriptions of my morning coffee ritual, the way I chewed my lip when concentrating, the specific, slightly clumsy way I tie my shoelaces. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, a sensation that made my jaw clench. I slowly lowered the book, the sound of the turning page seeming deafening in the silence. "Darling," I began, my voice unnaturally level, "what exactly are you writing?" The shift in his posture—the sudden stillness—was a flashing warning light.

[story 2]

I found the draft tucked inside a rarely used journal. The prose was intoxicating, a vivid portrait of my anxieties and small triumphs, written from an impossibly intimate perspective. My hands trembled as I traced a line describing a moment of profound vulnerability I hadn't even realized I’d shared. Every sentence felt like an intrusion, a careful dissection of my private landscape. I retreated to the bedroom, the air thick with unspoken tension. I watched him across the space, his expression carefully blank, a practiced mask settling over his features. I took a slow, deliberate breath, trying to keep my heart rate from accelerating. When he finally looked up, his gaze held a strange mixture of defiance and weary acceptance. "It’s just a story," he murmured, his tone smooth as polished stone, but the tremor in his hand as he reached for his glass of water betrayed him.

[story 3]

The discovery happened during a routine tidying session. A misplaced folder, labeled cryptically, led me to the truth: a sprawling narrative dedicated entirely to my existence. I felt a sudden, intense need to examine every page, to catalogue the sheer audacity of the project. My fingers moved across the paper, tracing the flow of his narrative, feeling a prickle of unease run down my arms. I didn't confront him immediately. Instead, I started moving around the apartment, checking locks, listening to the sounds of the street below, cataloging every slight deviation from the normal rhythm of our shared life. The quiet hum of the refrigerator suddenly sounded like a ticking clock. When I finally approached him, my voice was low, edged with a brittle quality. "Tell me," I managed, my eyes fixed on the screen, "how much of me is in those pages?"
